intro: The pivotal question between Anthropic and Cohere is how much of your setup stays portable: Anthropic carries low lock-in and Cohere carries medium lock-in. Anthropic is Claude LLM API and Claude.ai consumer product from Anthropic. Cohere is LLM API focused on enterprise text, embeddings, and RAG. Pick Anthropic when the job is need strong reasoning and long context (200k tokens) and you accept low lock-in; pick Cohere when it is api gateway and rate limiting and you accept medium lock-in. The data we have shows Anthropic at fully public pricing and documentation with a polished developer experience, and Cohere at mostly transparent pricing and docs with a polished developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Anthropic is a poor fit for need cheapest inference, and Cohere is a poor fit for internal, simple projects. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list.
quick_take: Anthropic is for Claude LLM API and Claude.ai consumer product; Cohere is for LLM API focused on enterprise text; decide based on how portable the setup must stay.
verdict_a: Choose Anthropic if a hybrid plan-plus-usage structure ($20/mo (Pro), API $3/$15 per 1M tokens (Sonnet)) fits your spending pattern and the work maps to Claude LLM API and Claude.ai consumer product from Anthropic.
verdict_b: Choose Cohere if some ecosystem coupling is acceptable and the work lines up with LLM API focused on enterprise text, embeddings, and RAG.

faq.a: Anthropic starts at $20/mo (Pro), API $3/$15 per 1M tokens (Sonnet) on a hybrid model; Cohere starts at $4.00 on a subscription model. That makes the sticker comparison depend on usage — a low entry price can get expensive once volume ramps, so price at your actual workload.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Anthropic to Cohere?
faq.a: Migration is possible in either direction. Anthropic carries low lock-in and Cohere carries medium lock-in, so plan for meaningful but not blocking rework. Run both in parallel before you fully cut over.

Both Anthropic and Cohere rate the same on developer experience (5/5). The decision on DX then comes down to taste — which CLI, UI, or workflow matches your team's habits. A short side-by-side trial is the quickest way to tell.

faq.q: Is Cohere a good alternative to Anthropic?
faq.a: Yes — Cohere is a reasonable alternative to Anthropic for need strong reasoning and long context (200k tokens). The practical differences are hybrid-vs-subscription billing and low-vs-medium lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Cohere as a credible swap.
